game_name: Nazo no Murasamejō (The Mysterious Murasame Castle)

design_philosophy.core_loop: Infiltrate four thematically-linked castles, defeat castle lords to obtain gems, then face final alien boss. Each level split into two parts: overworld traversal and castle interior combat.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Skill-based action combat with limited power-ups and time pressure; reliance on katana/shuriken mastery rather than item abundance. Health gauge and time limit create dual failure states.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Samurai apprentice on secret shogunate mission; infiltrating enemy castles in Edo-period Japan to combat supernatural corruption
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately sparse power-up design philosophy—game forces reliance on player execution skills rather than progression-based equipment scaling. Top-down directional movement (no side-scrolling) is unconventional for 1986 platformer paradigm. Weapon system enforces distance-based decision making (katana for close range, shurikens for distance).

difficulty.lives_system: Players start with limited lives; additional lives can be earned by rescuing princesses and completing bonus rounds after first half of each level. Reaching 99+ lives grants temporary invincibility state.
difficulty.continues_system: Game over screen allows player to continue or save progress when all lives exhausted
difficulty.checkpointing: Levels divided into two parts (path to castle, castle interior); bonus rounds available after completing first half
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Limited agency—no difficulty select documented. However, life system provides soft scaling: skilled players can accumulate lives for buffer against punishment.

progression.upgrade_system: Shuriken upgrades available but lost upon death (penalty-based progression). Power-ups include fireballs (more powerful than shurikens), screen-clearing lightning explosives, and temporary invisibility cloaks. Katana is permanent but distance-locked.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Four sequential castles must be infiltrated in order (Aosame, Akasame, Ryokusame, Momosame) to obtain gems, unlocking access to final Murasame Castle
replayability_hooks: Bonus rounds after level completion encourage replaying for life accumulation
Scrolls scattered throughout castles incentivize exploration
High degree of difficulty invites skill-based replayability

world_building_techniques: Enemy character design borrows heavily from Japanese culture (samurai, ninja, hannya masks)
Setting in Edo-period Japan with authentic castle architecture and daimyo lords
Alien creature as supernatural corruption element—contrast with historical setting
Environmental storytelling through castle design progression and lord defeats
Overarching plot of shogunate investigation provides narrative framing

boss_design.philosophy: Castle lords serve as level culminations; each represents a corrupted daimyo under alien influence. Final boss is the alien entity itself. Boss encounters require reaching 'innermost region' of castles, suggesting escalating difficulty zones.
notable_bosses: Four castle lords (Aosame, Akasame, Ryokusame, Momosame) - each guards an evil power sphere
Alien entity in Murasame Castle - final boss, described as stone statue animated by alien creature

faction_indicators: Shogunate (player allegiance) - represents order and investigation
Four corrupted daimyo - represent localized evil power spheres
Alien entity - represents external cosmic threat corrupting hierarchy
danger_signals: Ninja armies summoned by corrupted lords
Monster spawning tied to possession by alien power
Golden object falling from sky (alien arrival)
Deafening shrieks from castle (supernatural corruption audible signal)
environmental_storytelling: Four distinct castles each corrupted by possession
Princesses imprisoned in castles (rescue mechanic tied to narrative)
Progressing from corrupted satellite castles to central Murasame Castle mirrors closing investigation
Enemy type variation per castle reflects thematic corruption
